title: 01. Functions
text:
By the end of this session, students should be able to:

describe functions verbally, numerically, visually, and algebraically
find domains, ranges, and graphs of functions of one real variable
work with piecewise functions, composite functions, one-to-one functions, and inverse functions
recognize periodicity, monotonicity, symmetry, and boundedness
know the basic elementary families: linear, polynomial, power, exponential, logarithmic, trigonometric, inverse trigonometric, and hyperbolic functions
use shifts, stretches, shrinks, reflections, absolute values, and \(f(|x|)\) to build new graphs from old graphs

title: 01. Functions
text:
By the end of this session, students should be able to:

describe functions verbally, numerically, visually, and algebraically
find domains, ranges, and graphs of functions of one real variable
work with piecewise functions, composite functions, one-to-one functions, and inverse functions
recognize periodicity, monotonicity, symmetry, and boundedness
know the basic elementary families: linear, polynomial, power, exponential, logarithmic, trigonometric, inverse trigonometric, and hyperbolic functions
use shifts, stretches, shrinks, reflections, absolute values, and \(f(|x|)\) to build new graphs from old graphs

title: 01. Functions
text:
Function Review Checklist
By the end of the function review, students should be able to do the following.

Functions and Graphs

Give examples of functions from formulas, tables, descriptions, and graphs.
State what it means for \(f:X\to E\) to be a function: each input \(x\in X\) has exactly one output \(f(x)\in E\).
Identify the independent variable, dependent variable, domain \(D(f)\), and range \(E(f)\).
Interpret the graph of \(f\) as the set of points \((x,f(x))\).
Find the domain and range of common formulas, including square-root expressions.
Evaluate and sketch piecewise-defined functions.
Compute compositions:

\[
(f\circ g)(x)=f(g(x)).
\]

Decide whether a function is one-to-one and find inverse functions when possible.



Basic Properties of Functions

Recognize periodic functions and use the identity

\[
f(x+T)=f(x)
\]
for a period \(T>0\).

Determine whether a function is increasing or decreasing on an interval.
Test symmetry:

\[
f(-x)=f(x) \quad \text{even}, \qquad f(-x)=-f(x) \quad \text{odd}.
\]

Decide whether a function is bounded above, bounded below, bounded, or unbounded on a given domain.



Elementary Functions

Linear functions: know \(y=mx+b\), interpret \(m\) as slope, and find equations of lines.
Polynomials: recognize degree, leading coefficient, and common linear/quadratic/cubic cases.
Power functions: know how \(y=x^\alpha\) changes with \(\alpha\), including \(x^2\), \(1/x\), and \(\sqrt{x}\).
Exponential functions: know \(y=a^x\) for \(a>0\), \(a\neq 1\), their domains, ranges, monotonicity, and laws of exponents.
Logarithmic functions: know \(y=\log_a x\) as the inverse of \(y=a^x\), its domain/range, monotonicity, and log laws.
Trigonometric functions: know the basic properties of \(\sin x\), \(\cos x\), \(\tan x\), and \(\cot x\), including domains, ranges, periods, symmetry, and basic identities.
Inverse trigonometric functions: know the defining restrictions for \(\arcsin x\), \(\arccos x\), and \(\arctan x\).
Hyperbolic functions: know the definitions of \(\sinh x\), \(\cosh x\), \(\tanh x\), and \(\coth x\), plus the basic identity

\[
\cosh^2 x-\sinh^2 x=1.
\]


Transformations
Starting from the graph of \(y=f(x)\), students should know how to graph:

\(y=f(x)+c\) and \(y=f(x)-c\) by vertical shifts
\(y=f(x+c)\) and \(y=f(x-c)\) by horizontal shifts
\(y=cf(x)\) and \(y=\frac{1}{c}f(x)\) by vertical stretch/shrink
\(y=f(cx)\) and \(y=f(x/c)\) by horizontal shrink/stretch
\(y=-f(x)\) and \(y=f(-x)\) by reflections
\(y=|f(x)|\) and \(y=f(|x|)\) by reflecting selected parts of the graph

title: 01. Functions
text:
Active Prelude to Calculus Summary
Active Prelude to Calculus is a pre-calculus bridge text by Matthew Boelkins. For this course, it is most useful as the reading source for function review before limits and derivatives.
The book is organized around five main blocks:

Relating Changing Quantities: functions as models, average rate of change, linear functions, quadratic functions, composition, inverse functions, transformations, combining functions, and piecewise functions.
Circular Functions: the unit circle, sine and cosine, radians, sinusoidal functions, and periodic behavior.
Exponential and Logarithmic Functions: exponential growth and decay, modeling, the number \(e\), logarithms, log properties, and applications such as temperature and population.
Trigonometry: right-triangle trigonometry, tangent, inverse trigonometric functions, angle-finding, secant/cosecant/cotangent, and basic identities.
Polynomial and Rational Functions: power functions, polynomial behavior, zeros and signs, polynomial modeling, rational functions, domains, holes, and long-range behavior.

Active Prelude Chapters 1-5 support most of the function review. Two topics need extra treatment in these course notes: boundedness as a formal property of functions and hyperbolic functions.

title: 01. Functions
text:
Core Examples
A function is a rule that assigns each input exactly one output. In modeling, the hardest part is often not evaluating a formula, but deciding what the input and output should represent.
For functions \(f\) and \(g\), the composition
\[
(f \circ g)(x) = f(g(x))
\]
means that \(g\) acts first and \(f\) acts second.

title: 01. Functions
text:
Warm-Up
Suppose the temperature of a metal plate at time \(t\) is \(T(t)\), and the resistance of a sensor at temperature \(T\) is \(R(T)\).

What does \(R(T(t))\) represent?
What is the input variable?
What units should the output have?

title: 01. Functions
text:
Example
Let
\[
f(x)=\sqrt{x+4}, \qquad g(x)=x^2-1.
\]
Then
\[
(f \circ g)(x)=f(x^2-1)=\sqrt{x^2+3}.
\]
The formula is defined for all real \(x\), because \(x^2+3>0\).

title: 01. Functions
text:
Hyperbolic Functions
Hyperbolic functions are built from exponential functions. They appear naturally in differential equations, geometry, and several engineering models.

Definitions
The hyperbolic sine and hyperbolic cosine are defined by
\[
\sinh x=\frac{e^x-e^{-x}}{2},
\qquad
\cosh x=\frac{e^x+e^{-x}}{2}.
\]
The hyperbolic tangent and hyperbolic cotangent are defined by
\[
\tanh x=\frac{\sinh x}{\cosh x}
=\frac{e^x-e^{-x}}{e^x+e^{-x}},
\]
and
\[
\coth x=\frac{\cosh x}{\sinh x}
=\frac{e^x+e^{-x}}{e^x-e^{-x}},
\qquad x\neq 0.
\]


Basic Values
From the definitions,
\[
\sinh 0=0,\qquad \cosh 0=1,\qquad \tanh 0=0.
\]
The function \(\coth x\) is not defined at \(x=0\), since \(\sinh 0=0\).


Domain, Range, and Shape











Function
Domain
Range
Symmetry
Basic behavior




\(\sinh x\)
\(\mathbb{R}\)
\(\mathbb{R}\)
odd
increasing on \(\mathbb{R}\)


\(\cosh x\)
\(\mathbb{R}\)
\([1,\infty)\)
even
decreases on \((-\infty,0)\), increases on \((0,\infty)\)


\(\tanh x\)
\(\mathbb{R}\)
\((-1,1)\)
odd
increasing on \(\mathbb{R}\)


\(\coth x\)
\(\mathbb{R}\setminus\{0\}\)
\((-\infty,-1)\cup(1,\infty)\)
odd
undefined at \(0\); horizontal asymptotes \(y=\pm 1\)






Identities
The most important identity is
\[
\cosh^2 x-\sinh^2 x=1.
\]
This follows directly from the definitions:
\[
\cosh^2 x-\sinh^2 x
=\left(\frac{e^x+e^{-x}}{2}\right)^2
-\left(\frac{e^x-e^{-x}}{2}\right)^2
=1.
\]
Other useful identities include
\[
\sinh(-x)=-\sinh x,\qquad \cosh(-x)=\cosh x,
\]
\[
\sinh(x+y)=\sinh x\cosh y+\cosh x\sinh y,
\]
and
\[
\cosh(x+y)=\cosh x\cosh y+\sinh x\sinh y.
\]


Example
Simplify
\[
\cosh^2 x-\tanh^2 x\cosh^2 x.
\]
Since \(\tanh x=\sinh x/\cosh x\),
\[
\tanh^2 x\cosh^2 x=\sinh^2 x.
\]
Therefore,
\[
\cosh^2 x-\tanh^2 x\cosh^2 x
=\cosh^2 x-\sinh^2 x
=1.
\]

title: 01. Functions
text:
Reading And Practice

Active Prelude to Calculus: Chapters 1-5 as needed; especially Chapter 1 for functions, composition, inverses, and transformations.
OpenStax: review functions, graphs, basic classes of functions, trigonometric functions, inverse functions, exponential functions, and logarithmic functions.
Stewart: selected review problems on domains and composition.
Next class: read OpenStax Calculus Volume 2, Section 5.1: Sequences.

title: Readings
text:
Active Prelude to Calculus / Active Calculus: lecture preparation, conceptual questions, and in-class activities.
OpenStax Calculus: primary student reading and routine practice.
Stewart Calculus: additional practice, especially computational fluency and exam-style problems.
Instructor notes: local coverage for sequences, parametric curves, advanced integration techniques, and the ODE topics beyond the single-variable Active Calculus core.

title: Readings
text:
Reading Pattern
Each session should have a compact reading assignment:

A short pre-class section from Active Prelude, Active Calculus, or instructor notes.
A matching OpenStax section for textbook-style explanations.
A small Stewart problem set for optional extra practice.

title: Readings
text:
Suggested Student Instructions
Before class, students should read for the main idea rather than mastery. They should bring:

one question about a definition
one worked example they can explain
one problem where they got stuck

title: Calculus 1
text:
This site is a lightweight teaching spine for MT1003 Calculus 1. It is meant to keep lectures focused: short notes, active-learning prompts, reading assignments, and slide decks.
The main lecture source is Active Calculus. OpenStax and Stewart are used for reading, reference, and practice.


Course meetings

Time
Room

B4-505


DT02
Tue-Thu
12:00-14:50
C5-HOITRUONG



Course rhythm
Before class: read a short section and try warm-up questions.
In class: work through active prompts, examples, and quick checks.
After class: practice with OpenStax and Stewart problems.

title: Calculus 1
text:
Computing Software
We will use Julia for numerical experiments, plotting, and small computational checks. Please install Julia using the official installation instructions; Windows is supported there along with macOS and Linux.

title: Calculus 1
text:
AI Tools
You are entirely responsible for your own learning. Use AI tools with caution, and make sure that you can understand, explain, and complete the work properly without AI.

title: Calculus 1
text:
Course Focus
The syllabus emphasizes single-variable calculus and ordinary differential equations for engineering students:

functions, sequences, limits, and continuity
derivatives, differentials, Taylor expansions, and curve sketching
indefinite and definite integrals
applications of integration
improper integrals
first-order and second-order differential equations
systems of first-order linear differential equations

title: Calculus 1
text:
Assessment Anchors

Midterm exam: 30 percent
Major assignment / project: 30 percent
Final exam: 40 percent

The project component is a good place to connect calculus to engineering models, numerical exploration, and communication.

title: Calculus 1
text:
How To Use This Site
Use Schedule as the weekly map. Use Notes for short written summaries and Slides for in-class decks.
The short notes are intentionally lightweight. They point students to the right reading and practice while keeping class time active.

title: Project
text: The syllabus assigns a major project component worth 30 percent of the course grade. The project should reinforce the course goal: using single-variable calculus and differential equations to model practical problems.

title: Project
text:
Recommended Project Shape
Students work in groups and submit:

a short problem statement
a mathematical model
calculations or simulations
interpretation of results
a brief presentation

title: Project
text:
Possible Themes
Students may choose one of the following topics or propose their own:

optimization in engineering design
accumulated change and numerical integration
physical applications of definite integrals
population or mixing models with first-order ODEs
spring-mass or circuit models with second-order ODEs

title: Project
text:
Evaluation Ideas



Category
Weight




Mathematical model
30 percent


Correct calculations
25 percent


Interpretation and communication
25 percent


Presentation and teamwork
20 percent

title: 02. Sequences
text:
By the end of this session, students should be able to:

view a sequence as a function whose inputs are positive integers
write simple sequences using explicit formulas and recurrence relations
recognize arithmetic and geometric sequences
determine basic convergence and divergence behavior
use limit laws, the squeeze theorem, boundedness, and monotonicity for sequences

The sequence notes below are adapted from OpenStax Calculus Volume 2, Section 5.1: Sequences.

title: 02. Sequences
text:
By the end of this session, students should be able to:

view a sequence as a function whose inputs are positive integers
write simple sequences using explicit formulas and recurrence relations
recognize arithmetic and geometric sequences
determine basic convergence and divergence behavior
use limit laws, the squeeze theorem, boundedness, and monotonicity for sequences

The sequence notes below are adapted from OpenStax Calculus Volume 2, Section 5.1: Sequences.

title: 02. Sequences
text:
Definitions
An infinite sequence is an ordered list
\[
a_1,\ a_2,\ a_3,\ \ldots
\]
The number \(a_n\) is the \(n\)th term, and \(n\) is the index. A sequence can be viewed as a function whose domain is the positive integers.
A sequence may be defined explicitly:
\[
a_n = f(n),
\]
or recursively, by giving initial term(s) and a rule that produces later terms from earlier terms.
An arithmetic sequence has a constant difference:
\[
a_n = a_1 + (n-1)d.
\]
A geometric sequence has a constant ratio:
\[
a_n = a_1 r^{n-1}.
\]

title: 02. Sequences
text:
Convergence
A sequence \(\{a_n\}\) converges to \(L\) if the terms eventually become arbitrarily close to \(L\). Formally, for every \(\varepsilon>0\), there is an integer \(N\) such that
\[
|a_n-L|<\varepsilon \quad \text{whenever } n\geq N.
\]
In this case we write
\[
\lim_{n\to\infty} a_n = L.
\]
If no finite limit exists, the sequence diverges. For example, a sequence may diverge because its terms grow without bound, or because its terms oscillate without approaching one value.

title: 02. Sequences
text:
Useful Theorems
If \(a_n=f(n)\) and
\[
\lim_{x\to\infty} f(x)=L,
\]
then
\[
\lim_{n\to\infty} a_n=L.
\]
If \(\lim a_n=A\) and \(\lim b_n=B\), then the usual algebraic limit laws apply to sums, differences, constant multiples, products, and quotients, provided the quotient limit does not divide by zero.
If \(a_n\to L\) and \(f\) is continuous at \(L\), then
\[
f(a_n)\to f(L).
\]
The squeeze theorem also applies to sequences: if \(a_n\leq b_n\leq c_n\) eventually, and both \(a_n\) and \(c_n\) converge to \(L\), then \(b_n\) also converges to \(L\).

title: 02. Sequences
text:
Bounded And Monotone Sequences
A sequence is bounded above if all its terms are less than or equal to some fixed number. It is bounded below if all its terms are greater than or equal to some fixed number. A bounded sequence is both bounded above and bounded below.
A sequence is increasing if
\[
a_n \leq a_{n+1}
\]
for all relevant \(n\), and decreasing if
\[
a_n \geq a_{n+1}.
\]
An increasing or decreasing sequence is called monotone.
Every convergent sequence is bounded, but a bounded sequence need not converge. For instance,
\[
a_n=(-1)^n
\]
is bounded but divergent.
The Monotone Convergence Theorem says that a bounded sequence that is eventually monotone must converge.

title: 02. Sequences
text:
Examples
The sequence
\[
2,\ 4,\ 8,\ 16,\ \ldots
\]
can be written explicitly as \(a_n=2^n\) for \(n\geq 1\), or recursively as \(a_1=2\) and
\[
a_{n+1}=2a_n.
\]
The sequence
\[
a_n=\frac{n}{n+1}
\]
converges to \(1\), because the related function \(f(x)=x/(x+1)\) has limit \(1\) as \(x\to\infty\).
The sequence
\[
a_n=\frac{(-1)^n}{n}
\]
converges to \(0\). Indeed,
\[
0\leq \left|\frac{(-1)^n}{n}\right|\leq \frac{1}{n},
\]
and \(1/n\to 0\).
The sequence
\[
a_n=1-\frac{1}{n}
\]
is increasing and bounded above by \(1\), so it converges by the Monotone Convergence Theorem. Its limit is \(1\).

title: 02. Sequences
text:
Bridge To Limits
Sequences are a natural first place to ask what it means to approach a value. After this session, we will use the same idea for functions of a real variable.

title: 02. Sequences
text:
Reading And Practice

OpenStax: Calculus Volume 2, Section 5.1: Sequences.
Stewart: Section 11.1 for additional sequence practice.
Next reading: Active Calculus 1.1-1.2 and OpenStax Calculus Volume 1, Sections 2.1-2.2 for limits of functions.

Source note: The sequence definitions, theorem list, and example types are adapted from OpenStax Calculus Volume 2, Section 5.1: Sequences by Gilbert Strang and Edwin “Jed” Herman, OpenStax, licensed under CC BY-NC-SA 4.0.

title: Calculus 1
text:
Functions

MT1003 Calculus 1 - Session 01


Lecturer: Truong-Son Van tsvan@hcmut.edu.vn


Reading map: course readings.

title: Calculus 1
text:
Why Calculus?


The short answer

Calculus is the language for describing change, accumulation, and optimization.




Motion

How does position change with time?



Fields

How do quantities vary from point to point?



Learning

How do we improve a model by changing its parameters?



Motivation: functions are the objects that change; calculus measures and controls that change.

title: Calculus 1
text:
Navier-Stokes: Fluid Motion







Fluid mechanics

\[
\rho\left(\frac{\partial \mathbf u}{\partial t}
+(\mathbf u\cdot\nabla)\mathbf u\right)
=-\nabla p+\mu\Delta\mathbf u+\mathbf f
\]
The velocity field \(\mathbf u(x,y,z,t)\) changes in time and space. Calculus measures acceleration, pressure gradients, and diffusion of momentum.


Background: fluid simulation video, YouTube.

title: Calculus 1
text:
Maxwell: Fields And Waves







Electromagnetism

\[
\nabla\cdot\mathbf E=\frac{\rho}{\varepsilon_0},
\qquad
\nabla\times\mathbf B-\frac1{c^2}\frac{\partial\mathbf E}{\partial t}
=\mu_0\mathbf J
\]
Electric and magnetic fields are functions on space and time. The symbols \(\nabla\) and \(\frac{\partial}{\partial t}\) describe how those fields vary.


Background: Maxwell equation video, YouTube.

title: Calculus 1
text:
Einstein: Space-Time Curvature






Relativity

\[
G_{\mu\nu}+\Lambda g_{\mu\nu}
=\frac{8\pi G}{c^4}T_{\mu\nu}
\]
Geometry tells matter how to move; matter tells geometry how to curve. Calculus enters through curvature, rates of change, and differential geometry.


Background: BH Accretion Disk Sim 360 Continuous, NASA Goddard Space Flight Center/Jeremy Schnittman, public domain.

title: Calculus 1
text:
Schrodinger: Quantum Waves







Quantum mechanics

\[
i\hbar\frac{\partial\psi}{\partial t}
=\widehat H\psi
\]
The wave function \(\psi\) evolves according to a differential equation. Here calculus describes how probability waves move, spread, and interfere.


Background: quantum simulation video, YouTube.

title: Calculus 1
text:
AI: Learning By Gradients







Optimization and backpropagation

\[
L(\theta)=\frac1N\sum_{i=1}^N
\ell\big(f_\theta(x_i),y_i\big),
\qquad
\theta_{k+1}=\theta_k-\eta\nabla_\theta L(\theta_k)
\]
Backpropagation is repeated use of the chain rule to compute gradients efficiently. Modern AI learns by following those gradients.


Background: neural-network video, YouTube.

title: Calculus 1
text:
What Calculus 1 Starts Building


1
Functions
inputs, outputs, models


2
Limits
approach and approximation


3
Derivatives
instantaneous change


4
Integrals
accumulated change




Today

We begin with functions because every equation above describes relationships between changing quantities.


Bridge: functions first, then limits, derivatives, integrals, and differential equations.

title: Calculus 1
text:
Today



Core ideas


Functions as dependence
Domain, range, and graph
Piecewise, composite, one-to-one, and inverse functions




Main examples


Function properties
Elementary functions
Hyperbolic functions
Transformations





Next class

Sequences begin next class, after the function review is solid.


Read: Active Prelude 1.2-1.9; OpenStax 1.1-1.5.

title: Calculus 1
text:
Course Rhythm



Before class

Read the assigned sections and try the warm-up questions.



In class

Work through prompts, examples, and quick checks.



After class

Practice with OpenStax and Stewart.



Read: course readings; practice from OpenStax and Stewart sections listed there.

title: Calculus 1
text:
Functions Arise From Dependence



Formula

Circle area depends on radius:
\[A=\pi r^2.\]



Table

World population depends on time:
\[P=P(t).\]



Rule

Mailing cost depends on envelope weight:
\[C=C(w).\]



Graph

Ground acceleration during an earthquake depends on elapsed time:
\[a=a(t).\]



Theory: Active Prelude 1.2; OpenStax 1.1; Stewart 1.1.

title: Calculus 1
section: Four Ways To Represent A Function
text:
Four Ways To Represent A Function


1
Verbally
description in words


2
Numerically
table of values


3
Visually
graph


4
Algebraically
formula



Theory: Active Prelude 1.2; OpenStax 1.1; Stewart 1.1.

title: Calculus 1
text:
Function, Domain, Range


Definition

A function \(f\) assigns to each input \(x\) in a set \(X\) exactly one output \(f(x)\).
\[
f:X\to E, \qquad y=f(x)
\]




Domain

\[
D(f)=\{x\in \mathbb{R}: f(x)\text{ is defined}\}.
\]



Range

\[
E(f)=\{f(x):x\in D(f)\}.
\]



Theory: Active Prelude 1.2; OpenStax 1.1; Stewart 1.1.

title: Calculus 1
text:
Graph Of A Function



Graph

The graph of \(f\) is the set of all points
\[
(x,f(x)), \qquad x\in D(f).
\]






Theory: Active Prelude 1.2; OpenStax 1.1; Stewart 1.1.

title: Calculus 1
text:
Example: Domain And Range


Problem

Find the domain and range of
\[f(x)=\sqrt{x+2}.\]



Solution

For \(f(x)\) to be defined,
\[
x+2\geq 0 \Longleftrightarrow x\geq -2.
\]
Therefore
\[
D(f)=[-2,\infty), \qquad E(f)=[0,\infty).
\]


Practice: Active Prelude 1.2; OpenStax 1.1; Stewart 1.1.

title: Calculus 1
text:
Piecewise Defined Functions


Definition

A piecewise function uses different formulas on different parts of its domain.



Example

\[
f(x)=
\begin{cases}
1-x, & x\leq -1,\\
x^2, & x>-1.
\end{cases}
\]
Quick checks:
\[
f(-2)=3,\qquad f(-1)=2,\qquad f(2)=4.
\]


Theory: Active Prelude 1.2; OpenStax 1.1; Stewart 1.1.

title: Calculus 1
text:
Composite Functions



Definition

If \(y=f(u)\) and \(u=g(x)\), then substitution gives
\[y=f(g(x)).\]
The composite function is
\[
(f\circ g)(x)=f(g(x)).
\]






Theory: Active Prelude 1.6; OpenStax 1.1; Stewart 1.3.

title: Calculus 1
text:
Example: Composition


Problem

Let
\[
f(x)=x^2, \qquad g(x)=x^3-7.
\]
Find \(f\circ g\) and \(g\circ f\).




First order

\[
(f\circ g)(x)=f(g(x))=(x^3-7)^2.
\]



Reverse order

\[
(g\circ f)(x)=g(f(x))=x^6-7.
\]



Practice: Active Prelude 1.6; OpenStax 1.1; Stewart 1.3.

title: Calculus 1
text:
One-To-One Functions


Definition

A function \(f\) is one-to-one if it never takes the same value twice:
\[
x_1\neq x_2 \Longrightarrow f(x_1)\neq f(x_2).
\]




One-to-one

\(f(x)=x^3\) is one-to-one.



Not one-to-one

\(g(x)=x^2\) is not one-to-one because
\[g(1)=1=g(-1).\]



Theory: Active Prelude 1.7; OpenStax 1.4; Stewart 1.5.

title: Calculus 1
text:
Inverse Functions


Definition

Let \(f\) be one-to-one with domain \(D\) and range \(E\).
The inverse function \(f^{-1}\) has domain \(E\) and range \(D\), and
\[
f^{-1}(y)=x \Longleftrightarrow f(x)=y.
\]



Recipe


Write \(y=f(x)\).
Solve for \(x\) in terms of \(y\).
Interchange \(x\) and \(y\).



Theory: Active Prelude 1.7; OpenStax 1.4; Stewart 1.5.

title: Calculus 1
text:
Example: Inverse Function


Problem

Find the inverse of
\[f(x)=x^3+2.\]



Solution

\[
y=x^3+2
\]
\[
x^3=y-2
\]
\[
x=(y-2)^{1/3}
\]
After interchanging \(x\) and \(y\):
\[
f^{-1}(x)=(x-2)^{1/3}.
\]


Practice: Active Prelude 1.7; OpenStax 1.4; Stewart 1.5.

title: Calculus 1
text:
Basic Properties



Domain

Where is it defined?



Range

What can it output?



Periodicity

Does it repeat?



Monotonicity

Is it increasing or decreasing?



Symmetry

Is it even or odd?



Boundedness

Can the values escape every bound?



Theory: Active Prelude 1.2; OpenStax 1.1; Stewart 1.1.

title: Calculus 1
text:
Periodic Functions


Definition

A function \(f\) is periodic with period \(T>0\) if
\[
f(x+T)=f(x-T)=f(x)
\]
whenever the expressions are defined.



Example

\(f(x)=\sin x\) has period \(2\pi\) because
\[
\sin(x+2\pi)=\sin(x-2\pi)=\sin x.
\]


Theory: OpenStax 1.3; Stewart 1.3.

title: Calculus 1
text:
Increasing And Decreasing



Increasing

\[
x_1<x_2 \Longrightarrow f(x_1)<f(x_2).
\]



Decreasing

\[
x_1<x_2 \Longrightarrow f(x_1)>f(x_2).
\]




Examples


\(f(x)=x\) is increasing on \(\mathbb{R}\).
\(g(x)=x^2\) is decreasing on \((-\infty,0)\) and increasing on \((0,\infty)\).
\(h(x)=c\) is neither increasing nor decreasing under the strict definition.



Theory: Active Prelude 1.2; OpenStax 1.1; Stewart 1.1.

title: Calculus 1
text:
Symmetry



Even

\[f(-x)=f(x).\]
The graph is symmetric about the \(y\)-axis.



Odd

\[f(-x)=-f(x).\]
The graph is symmetric about the origin.




Examples

\(f(x)=x\) is odd. \(g(x)=x^2\) is even.


Theory: OpenStax 1.1; OpenStax 1.2; Stewart 1.1.

title: Calculus 1
text:
Boundedness



Definitions


bounded above: \(f(x)\leq M\)
bounded below: \(f(x)\geq m\)
bounded: \(|f(x)|\leq C\)
unbounded: no single \(C\) works




Examples

\[
f(x)=\frac{1}{1+x^2}
\]
is bounded on \(\mathbb{R}\) because \(0<f(x)\leq 1\).
\[
g(x)=\frac{1}{x}
\]
is bounded on \([1,\infty)\) but unbounded on \((0,\infty)\).




Why \(1/x\) is unbounded near \(0\)

For any \(C>0\), choose \(x_0=1/(C+1)\). Then \(x_0\in(0,\infty)\) and \(|g(x_0)|=C+1>C\).


Theory: OpenStax 1.1; OpenStax 1.2; Stewart 1.1.

title: Calculus 1
text:
Linear Functions


Definition

A linear function has the form
\[y=mx+b.\]
\(m\) is the slope and \(b\) is the \(y\)-intercept.



Slope

For two points \((x_1,y_1)\) and \((x_2,y_2)\) with \(x_1\neq x_2\),
\[
m=\frac{y_2-y_1}{x_2-x_1}.
\]


Theory: Active Prelude 1.4; OpenStax 1.2; Stewart 1.2.

title: Calculus 1
text:
Example: Line Through Two Points


Problem

Find the slope and equation of the line through \((4,3)\) and \((2,5)\).



Solution

\[
m=\frac{5-3}{2-4}=-1.
\]
Using point \((4,3)\):
\[
y-3=-1(x-4)
\]
so
\[
y=-x+7.
\]


Practice: Active Prelude 1.4; OpenStax 1.2; Stewart 1.2.

title: Calculus 1
text:
Polynomials


Definition

\[
P(x)=a_nx^n+a_{n-1}x^{n-1}+\cdots+a_1x+a_0.
\]
If \(a_n\neq 0\), the degree is \(n\).




Degree 1

\(P(x)=mx+b\)



Degree 2

\(P(x)=ax^2+bx+c\)



Degree 3

\(P(x)=ax^3+bx^2+cx+d\)



Theory: Active Prelude 1.5; OpenStax 1.2; Stewart 1.2.

title: Calculus 1
text:
Power Functions


Definition

A power function has the form
\[
y=x^\alpha, \qquad \alpha\in\mathbb{R}.
\]
The domain, range, monotonicity, and symmetry depend on \(\alpha\).


Theory: OpenStax 1.2; Stewart 1.2.

title: Calculus 1
text:
Example: Square Function



\(y=x^2\)


Domain: \(\mathbb{R}\)
Range: \([0,\infty)\)
Decreasing on \((-\infty,0)\)
Increasing on \((0,\infty)\)
Even







Practice: Active Prelude 1.5; OpenStax 1.2; Stewart 1.2.

title: Calculus 1
text:
Example: Reciprocal Function



\(y=1/x\)


Domain: \(\mathbb{R}\setminus\{0\}\)
Range: \((-\infty,0)\cup(0,\infty)\)
Decreasing on \((-\infty,0)\) and \((0,\infty)\)
Odd







Practice: OpenStax 1.2; Stewart 1.2.

title: Calculus 1
text:
Example: Square Root Function



\(y=\sqrt{x}\)


Domain: \([0,\infty)\)
Range: \([0,\infty)\)
Increasing on \((0,\infty)\)
No even/odd symmetry







Practice: OpenStax 1.2; Stewart 1.2.

title: Calculus 1
text:
Exponential Functions


Definition

\[
y=a^x,\qquad a>0,\quad a\neq 1.
\]




Laws

\[a^x a^y=a^{x+y},\qquad \frac{a^x}{a^y}=a^{x-y}\] \[\left(a^x\right)^y=a^{xy},\qquad a^{-x}=\frac{1}{a^x}\]



More laws

\[a^x b^x=(ab)^x,\qquad
\frac{a^x}{b^x}=\left(\frac{a}{b}\right)^x\]



Theory: OpenStax 1.5; Stewart 1.4.

title: Calculus 1
text:
Examples: Exponential Graphs



\(a>1\)


Domain: \(\mathbb{R}\)
Range: \((0,\infty)\)
Increasing on \(\mathbb{R}\)
Passes through \((0,1)\)





\(0 < a < 1\)


Domain: \(\mathbb{R}\)
Range: \((0,\infty)\)
Decreasing on \(\mathbb{R}\)
Passes through \((0,1)\)





Practice: OpenStax 1.5; Stewart 1.4.

title: Calculus 1
text:
Logarithmic Functions


Definition

\[
y=\log_a x,\qquad a>0,\quad a\neq 1.
\]
It is the inverse of \(y=a^x\):
\[
y=\log_a x \Longleftrightarrow x=a^y.
\]



Domain and range

\[
D=(0,\infty), \qquad E=\mathbb{R}.
\]


Theory: OpenStax 1.5; Stewart 1.5.

title: Calculus 1
text:
Logarithm Laws



Products and quotients

\[
\log_a(xy)=\log_a x+\log_a y
\]
\[
\log_a\left(\frac{x}{y}\right)=\log_a x-\log_a y
\]



Reciprocals and powers

\[
\log_a\left(\frac1x\right)=-\log_a x
\]
\[
\log_{a^\beta}x^\alpha=\frac{\alpha}{\beta}\log_a x
\]



Theory: OpenStax 1.5; Stewart 1.5.

title: Calculus 1
text:
Examples: Logarithmic Graphs



\(a>1\)


Increasing on \((0,\infty)\)
Reflection of \(y=a^x\) across \(y=x\)





\(0 < a < 1\)


Decreasing on \((0,\infty)\)
Reflection of \(y=a^x\) across \(y=x\)





Practice: OpenStax 1.5; Stewart 1.5.

title: Calculus 1
text:
Trigonometric Functions


Track the same features


domain
range
period
monotonicity on typical intervals
symmetry




Question

Which basic trigonometric functions are bounded?


Theory: OpenStax 1.3; Stewart 1.3.

title: Calculus 1
text:
Examples: Sine And Cosine



\(y=\sin x\)


Domain: \(\mathbb{R}\)
Range: \([-1,1]\)
Period: \(2\pi\)
Odd





\(y=\cos x\)


Domain: \(\mathbb{R}\)
Range: \([-1,1]\)
Period: \(2\pi\)
Even





Practice: OpenStax 1.3; Stewart 1.3.

title: Calculus 1
text:
Examples: Tangent And Cotangent



\(y=\tan x\)

\[y=\frac{\sin x}{\cos x}\]

Domain: \(\mathbb{R}\setminus\{\frac{\pi}{2}+k\pi:k\in\mathbb{Z}\}\)
Range: \(\mathbb{R}\)
Period: \(\pi\)
Odd





\(y=\cot x\)

\[y=\frac{\cos x}{\sin x}\]

Domain: \(\mathbb{R}\setminus\{k\pi:k\in\mathbb{Z}\}\)
Range: \(\mathbb{R}\)
Period: \(\pi\)
Odd





Practice: OpenStax 1.3; Stewart 1.3.

title: Calculus 1
text:
Basic Trigonometric Formulas



Sine

\[\sin^2x+\cos^2x=1\] \[\sin 2x=2\sin x\cos x\] \[\sin 3x=3\sin x-4\sin^3x\] \[\sin^2x=\frac{1-\cos 2x}{2}\]



Cosine

\[\cos 2x=\cos^2x-\sin^2x\] \[\cos 2x=2\cos^2x-1=1-2\sin^2x\] \[\cos^2x=\frac{1+\cos 2x}{2}\]



Theory: OpenStax 1.3; Stewart 1.3.

title: Calculus 1
text:
Inverse Trigonometric Functions



Arcsine

\[
y=\arcsin x \Longleftrightarrow x=\sin y
\]
\[
-1\leq x\leq 1,\quad -\frac{\pi}{2}\leq y\leq\frac{\pi}{2}
\]



Arccosine

\[
y=\arccos x \Longleftrightarrow x=\cos y
\]
\[
-1\leq x\leq 1,\quad 0\leq y\leq\pi
\]



Arctangent

\[
y=\arctan x \Longleftrightarrow x=\tan y
\]
\[
x\in\mathbb{R},\quad -\frac{\pi}{2}<y<\frac{\pi}{2}
\]



Theory: OpenStax 1.4; Stewart 1.5.

title: Calculus 1
text:
Hyperbolic Functions


Definitions

Hyperbolic functions are built from exponentials:
\[
\sinh x=\frac{e^x-e^{-x}}{2},\qquad
\cosh x=\frac{e^x+e^{-x}}{2}.
\]



Contrast

They are not periodic like sine and cosine, but they satisfy identities that look similar to trigonometric identities.


Theory: OpenStax 1.5 for exponentials; Stewart 3.11.

title: Calculus 1
text:
Examples: Hyperbolic Sine And Cosine



\(y=\sinh x\)


Domain: \(\mathbb{R}\)
Range: \(\mathbb{R}\)
Increasing on \(\mathbb{R}\)
Odd





\(y=\cosh x\)


Domain: \(\mathbb{R}\)
Range: \([1,\infty)\)
Decreasing on \((-\infty,0)\)
Increasing on \((0,\infty)\)
Even





Practice: OpenStax 1.5 for exponential structure; Stewart 3.11.

title: Calculus 1
text:
Hyperbolic Tangent And Cotangent



Definitions

\[
\tanh x=\frac{\sinh x}{\cosh x}
\]
\[
\coth x=\frac{\cosh x}{\sinh x}
\]



Domains


\(\tanh x\) is defined for all real \(x\).
\(\coth x\) is undefined at \(x=0\).




Theory: OpenStax 1.5 for exponentials; Stewart 3.11.

title: Calculus 1
text:
Hyperbolic Identities



Parity and fundamental identity

\[\sinh(-x)=-\sinh x\] \[\cosh(-x)=\cosh x\] \[\cosh^2x-\sinh^2x=1\]



Addition formulas

\[\cosh^2x+\sinh^2x=\cosh 2x\] \[\sinh(x+y)=\sinh x\cosh y+\cosh x\sinh y\] \[\cosh(x+y)=\cosh x\cosh y+\sinh x\sinh y\]



Theory: Stewart 3.11; compare identities with OpenStax 1.3.

title: Calculus 1
text:
New Functions From Old Functions



Shifts

For \(c>0\):

\(f(x)+c\): up
\(f(x)-c\): down
\(f(x+c)\): left
\(f(x-c)\): right




Stretches and reflections

For \(c>1\):

\(cf(x)\): vertical stretch
\(\frac1c f(x)\): vertical shrink
\(f(cx)\): horizontal shrink
\(f(\frac1c x)\): horizontal stretch
\(-f(x)\) and \(f(-x)\): reflections




Theory: Active Prelude 1.8; OpenStax 1.1; Stewart 1.3.

title: Calculus 1
text:
Example: Transform \(y=x^2\)


Problem

Given \(y=x^2\), graph:

\(y=f(x)=(x-2)^2-3\)
\(y=|f(x)|=|(x-2)^2-3|\)
\(y=f(|x|)=(|x|-2)^2-3\)



  


Practice: Active Prelude 1.8; OpenStax 1.1; Stewart 1.3.

title: Calculus 1
text:
Example: Transform \(y=\cos x\)


Problem

Given \(y=\cos x\), graph:

\(y=2\cos x\)
\(y=\frac12\cos x\)
\(y=\cos(2x)\)
\(y=\cos\left(\frac12 x\right)\)





Vertical transformations





Horizontal transformations





Practice: Active Prelude 1.8; OpenStax 1.3; Stewart 1.3.
